---
title: "YETI24 DIY Eye-Tracker Tutorial"
author: "Amira Shymbolatova"
date: "`r format(Sys.Date(), '%d %B %Y')`"
output:
  html_document:
    toc: true
    toc_depth: 3
    number_sections: true
    df_print: paged
editor_options: 
  markdown: 
    wrap: 72
---

# What you will build

This tutorial explains how to build and run YETI24, a low-cost
dual-camera eye-tracker prototype hosted inside the YET repository:

-   Eye-tracking software: yeti24/et/

-   IMU prototype + logging: yeti24/imu/

-   3D-printable parts: yeti24/cad/stl/ (and STEP in yeti24/cad/step/)

The goal is that another person can reproduce the setup easily in a few
hours (excluding 3D printing) by following the steps below.

# Repository structure

The relevant structure inside YET/yeti24/:

-   cad/

    -   stl/ — STL files to print

    -   step/ — STEP CAD exports

-   docs/ — documentation files (this tutorial lives here)

-   et/ — eye-tracker application

    -   Data/ — recordings / outputs (project-specific)

    -   Stimuli/ — calibration / stimuli files (project-specific)

    -   Config.csv — configuration file

    -   haarcascade_eye.xml — eye detection cascade

    -   libyeti24.py — eye-tracker library

    -   run.py — main entry point for the eye tracker

-   imu/ — IMU work

    -   pico/ — microcontroller firmware/scripts

    -   host/ — host-side scripts (PC/Mac)

    -   analysis/ — data exploration/analysis

    -   data/ — recorded IMU logs

    -   imu/README.md — IMU-specific notes

-   README.md — General notes

# Bill of Materials (BOM)

## Electronics

-   USB endoscope camera with 5.5 mm diameter and LED lights (The cable
    should be flexible and between 1.5 and 2m. You can find these for
    \<10 EUR at various resellers. Before you buy, check that the USB
    plug is compatible with your notebook computer)

-   2 x IR LEDs Modules for Raspberry Pi Camera (850 nm)

-   USB data cable with Micro-USB on one side, and on the other side,
    whatever fits into your computer

-   Power source for LEDs (USB 3W / battery pack / regulated supply)

-   Microcontroller board (suggested to use Raspberry Pi Pico)

-   IMU module (suggested to use LSM6DS)

## 3D-printed parts

STL files: yeti24/cad/stl/

Print the parts required by your build (example list — adjust to match
your final prototype):

-   Wire_cap × 8

-   Ir_led_clip × 2

-   Ir_led_case × 2

-   Hook_barebone_clip × 1

-   Grove_mount × 1

-   Et_mount.stl × 2

-   Barebone_clip.stl × 1

## Tools

-   Screwdriver

-   Pliers

-   Wires (3.5 mm x 5 meters)

-   (Optional) multimeter (recommended for LED wiring checks)

-   Preferred programming environment (recommended ZED)

# Step 1 — Print the parts

STL files are located here:

-   YET/yeti24/cad/stl/

Recommended print settings:

-   Layer height: 0.4 mm

Checkpoint: parts fit without forcing; cameras sit tightly; clips lock
as intended.

# Step 2 — Assemble the hardware 

![](Downloads/Picture1.jpg){width="300"}

1.  Measure two long wires, one that would serve as a headband (fold in
    half for stability) and the other that would hold the back of the
    head. Use Hook_barebone_clip and Barebone_clip.stl to mount them
    together. Two upper holes of the barebone should hold the headband
    wire; two lower holes of the barebone should hold the wire for the
    back of the head (ensure that the clips of the barebone look
    outwards from the head).

    1.  (Optional): On the headband wire, put on the Grove_mount to hold
        the IMU. Beforehand, screw the IMU to the Grove_mount.

2.  Measure two short wires, fold them in half and insert them through
    both of the barebones upper and lower center holes. At the end of
    both of these wires, put on the Et_mount (s).

    1.  (Optional): on both of the short wires put on before Et_mount
        (s), Ir_led_case (s) that are glued to Ir_led_clip (s).

3.  Insert the left camera into the left Et_mount. Insert the right
    camera into the right Et_mount. Clip both USB cameras to the
    barebones outer clip.

4.  (Optional) Cover the end of the wires with wire_cap (s)

5.  Ensure the cameras are rigid and aligned.

Checkpoint: lightly tapping the mount should not shift camera positions.

# Step 3 — Attach IR LEDs

IR LEDs stabilize eye image contrast and improve robustness of
pupil/feature detection, reducing sensitivity to ambient lighting.

## Mounting steps

1.  Place each IR LED into the corresponding Ir_led_case (using a small
    amount of glue).

2.  Glue the case with the IR LED in it to Ir_led_clip.

3.  Attach the LED modules near the cameras (preferably symmetric
    left/right).

4.  Route wiring safely away from the face and moving parts.

Checkpoint: LEDs power on and stay cool during operation.

## Safety note (IR LEDs)

Infrared (IR) LEDs can still be harmful even though the light is not
visible.

-   Do not stare into powered IR LEDs.

-   Use correct current limiting (resistor/driver) and verify LEDs do
    not heat up.

-   Avoid placing LEDs very close to the eyes or running them at
    excessive brightness.

# Step 4 — Software setup (running the eye tracker)

The eye tracker is launched from:

-   YET/yeti24/et/run.py

## Recommended run workflow

1.  Open Terminal.

2.  Install ppipenv: python3 -m pipenv shell

3.  Navigate to the eye-tracker folder: cd /path/to/YET/yeti24/et

4.  Run: python run.py

## Configuration file

The main configuration is stored in:

-   YET/yeti24/et/Config.csv

YETI24 is configured via code / Config.csv. Most settings work
out-of-the-box if you keep the default folder structure (et/Stimuli,
et/Data, etc.). The key parameters define: camera device IDs, experiment
metadata, stimulus paths, result output files, window/screen size, and
slide timing.

Most users only need to adjust:

-   USB_L and USB_R: left/right USB camera indices (systems with a
    built-in webcam often use index 0, so external cameras may start at
    1+).

-   EXP_ID: experiment identifier (used in filenames and saved results).

-   EXPERIMENTER: experimenter identifier (useful when multiple people
    collect data with different setups).

-   WIDTH, HEIGHT: window size in pixels (set slightly below your real
    screen resolution).

-   SLIDE_TIME: stimulus duration in seconds (increase for reading
    tasks, decrease for quick testing).

-   STIM_FILE: file containing stimuli for your experiment

## Calibration & quick validation

-   The eye-tracker includes two calibration routines:

    -   Initial calibration (training): the participant looks at nine
        target dots on the screen one-by-one to build the gaze mapping.

    -   Quick calibration: the participant looks at one dot in the
        center of the screen.

-   During an experiment, quick calibration can be used repeatedly to
    quickly correct offsets caused by small head or camera movements,
    which helps reduce drift from small movements

-   This approach reduces the need for full head tracking or strict head
    fixation, but it is still recommended to use:

    -   a stable head mount (e.g., mounted on headphones / rigid frame),
        and

    -   a simple headrest when possible.

Checkpoint: after calibration, the gaze point should move logically with
where the participant looks.

# Step 5 — IMU: running

IMU-related work is located in:

-   YET/yeti24/imu/

Open the IMU documentation and get familiar with it:

-   YET/yeti24/imu/README.md

Typical workflow

1.  Connect the IMU

    Connect the IMU to your machine (via USB serial through a
    microcontroller).

    Tip: On macOS, list available serial ports: ls /dev/tty.\*

2.  Download the code.py from the yeti24/imu/pico to the microcontroller

3.  Open Terminal

4.  Run: python3 -m pipenv shell

5.  Navigate to the IMU host folder: cd /path/to/YET/yeti24/imu/host

6.  Run: python log_imu.py

7.  Expected output: streaming timestamped gyroscope measurements; data
    saved into: YET/yeti24/imu/data/

8.  After the data collection open in RStudio, imu_pipeline.Rmd from
    yeti24/imu/analysis and follow the steps to filter the collected
    data

Checkpoint: a new log file appears, and values change when you move the
device.
